CIFAR-10 Classification project FCN¶

In [53]:
import keras
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, Dense, Flatten, Dropout, BatchNormalization
from tensorflow.keras.utils import to_categorical
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Data preparation¶

Load data and reshape it to be in the approriate form. Also scale the values to 0.0-1.0 for better performance

In [237]:
# Load data
(x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = keras.datasets.cifar10.load_data()

/home/oomis/anaconda3/envs/kerasgpu2/lib/python3.11/site-packages/keras/src/datasets/cifar.py:18: VisibleDeprecationWarning: dtype(): align should be passed as Python or NumPy boolean but got `align=0`. Did you mean to pass a tuple to create a subarray type? (Deprecated NumPy 2.4)
  d = cPickle.load(f, encoding="bytes")
In [3]:
print(x_train.shape, y_train.shape)
print(x_test.shape, y_test.shape)

(50000, 32, 32, 3) (50000, 1)
(10000, 32, 32, 3) (10000, 1)
In [9]:
# Reshape and scale to 0.0-1.0
x_train = x_train.reshape(50000, 32*32*3).astype("float32") /255
x_test = x_test.reshape(10000, 32*32*3).astype("float32") /255

Model¶

In [223]:
# Build layers
model = Sequential([
    Input(shape=(3072,)),
    Dense(1024, activation='relu'),
    BatchNormalization(),
    Dense(512, activation='relu'),
    BatchNormalization(),
    Dense(256, activation='relu'),
    BatchNormalization(),
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.5),
    Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])

# Model options
model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss=keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy(),
              metrics=['accuracy'],
)

history = model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=20, batch_size=64, validation_split=0.2)

In [227]:
test_scores = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test, verbose=2)
print("Test loss:", test_scores[0])
print("Test accuracy:", test_scores[1])

313/313 - 2s - 5ms/step - accuracy: 0.5113 - loss: 1.4286
Test loss: 1.428582787513733
Test accuracy: 0.5113000273704529

Model tuning methodology¶

Tried different setups for the model. Tried different amounts of layers and cells. Then tried different epochs to find more accurate models without too much overfitting. Started with fewer layers and less cells in the layers, but noticed an increase in accuracy when adding more layers and cells up to the point show above (4 hidden layers with 1028-512-256-128 cells respectively).
Added batch normalization which increased model accuracy by multiple percentage points, but it also made the validation loss graph more erratic. Dropout also improved performance by a little. Trying with more dropouts made the model more unstable.

Analysis¶

From the example prediction results below we see that the confidence of the predictions varies a lot. It seems to give >50% probabilites a small majority of the time for the top guess even if the guess is wrong.
For example, in picture 7 and 14 below the prediction is quite confidently wrong. In picture 14 it's quite understandable that planes and birds are easier to mix up in a picture where they are in flight, the angle is from above and the planes wings are not standard style airplane wings (I would also assume very few of the plane pictures are of a stealth bomber). In picture 7 There probably aren't many horse pictures where the horse is "upright" and also the model predicted some horse pictures as trucks (also picture 18).
There were more mixed results of animal picture where the angle/zoom etc. where not "standard" where it distributed the odds to multiple animals.

The comparison analysis between this model and the CNN-model will be in the CNN-models documentation.

In [236]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = x_test
y_pred = model(x)
start = 5000
labels = ["airplane","automobile","bird","cat","deer","dog","frog","horse","ship","truck"]

for k in range(20):
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 2))
    
    # Show image
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
    plt.imshow(x[start+k].reshape((32, 32, 3)))
    plt.axis("off")
    true_label_idx = int(y_test[start+k]) if y_test.ndim == 1 else int(y_test[start+k, 0])
    plt.title(f"{k+1}. {labels[true_label_idx]}")

    # Show bar graph
    plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
    probs = y_pred[start+k].numpy().flatten()

    # Color the correct label green
    colors = ["green" if i == true_label_idx else "blue" for i in range(10)]
    plt.bar(range(10), probs, color=colors)
    plt.xticks(range(10), labels, rotation=45)
    plt.ylim([0, 1])
    plt.title("Predicted probabilities")
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
In [228]:
# Save model
model.save('./CIFAR_10_FCN.keras')
